query: role of Groucho-related gene 5 (GRG5) in embryonic versus postnatal development

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Heart -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The establishment of laterality is an essential aspect of cardiac development, facilitated by cilia configuration within the primitive node. Ciliary structures found in the primitive node have a unique design that allows for overall leftward migration with the failure of such a process, leading to dextrocardia conditions. The motile cilia rotate in a clockwise fashion to generate this movement. The determination of the left-right cardiac axis is via several genes typically induced by NODAL expression. In turn, the NODAL expression induces expression of left-right determination factor ( LEFTY ) gene expression and paired-like homeodomain transcription factor 2 ( PITX2 ). [6] The PITX2 gene is considered the primary gene responsible for the determination of left-right orientation. The PITX2 gene has several roles in cardiac development. In addition to establishing left-right orientation, PITX2 gene mutations have associations with isolated cardiac congenital heart disease, including atrial septal defect, ventricular septal defect, and tetralogy of Fallot. [7] Even aside from mutations, some studies have emphasized the global effects of regulatory gene knockout on embryonic lethalities, such as the demonstrated importance of VangI2 on cardiac outflow tract development and Noggin on contractility. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Optic Fissure -- Mechanism -- Differentiation
retrieved_docs.content: As the optic cup develops from the bi-layer outpouching of neuroectoderm, it has several unique interactions with the adjacent tissues that cause specialized differentiation. The fibroblast growth factor of the surface ectoderm at the lens placode promotes inward differentiation of the neural retina. This is accomplished via FGF-induced downregulation of Mitf, allowing Vsx2 expression and creating a separation between the neural retina and the retinal pigmented epithelium at the optic fissure margin. Transforming growth factor B of the surrounding mesenchyme promotes outward differentiation of the pigmented layer of the retina, a process requiring WNT-B-Catenin signaling. The search clear delineation in retinal layers at this margin is key to the full fusion of the optic fissure. Failure of signaling processes, particularly mutation of WNT receptor gene FZD5, has been implicated in coloboma. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The ARX gene encodes for the aristaless related homeobox protein, which is a transcription factor with an important role in the forebrain and other tissue. [14] Children with ARX mutation present with other symptoms such as the absence of portions of the brain (corpus callosum agenesis), abnormal genitalia, and severe epilepsy. [15] [16] RELN RELN gene encodes reelin, an extracellular matrix glycoprotein. The mutation of this gene is involved in lissencephaly associated with cerebellar hypoplasia and hippocampal abnormalities. [17] Other genes: Several genes have established a relationship with lissencephaly. Those genes are VLDLR, ACTB, ACTG1, TUBG1, KIF5C, KIF2A, and CDK5. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Week 1 -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Blastocyst Formation: One of the first key developmental patterning processes in the morula to blastocyst transition is to determine what becomes inner cell mass vs. trophectoderm.  Studies in mice indicate that the upregulation of the transcription factors such as Oct 4, FGF, Nanog define pluripotent stem cells of the ICM, with trophectoderm cells upregulating Cdx-2 Eomes and Tead 4.

retrieved_docs.title: Ambiguous Genitalia and Disorders of Sexual Differentiation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: 1. SRY gene: the sex-determining region of the Y chromosome is the chief regulator of male sex differentiation; expression causes translation of SRY protein, which mediated testicular development. [8] 2.SOX9 gene: expression of this gene follows the SRY gene and is responsible for the differentiation of Sertoli cells. 3. NR5A1/SF-1: The steroidogenic factor gene codes an important transcription factor involved in male development and steroid biosynthesis. [9] [10] 4. DHH gene: The desert hedgehog gene plays a role in testicular differentiation. [11] 5. DAX/NROB1: Considered as an anti testis factor up-regulated in the ovary. 6.WT1: Codes a transcription factor involved in renal and gonadal development, mutation results in various congenital syndromes of abnormal genitourinary development. 7. Wnt4 and Wnt 7a: Wnt 4 suppresses male sexual differentiation and ovarian androgen production. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hand -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: At the molecular level, protein factors, such as ligands, receptors and transcription factors, play a critical role in the development of the limb. Sonic hedgehog (Shh) and fibroblast growth factors are specific ligands and serve important roles in cell signaling. These ligand factors induce differentiation and proliferation of neighboring cell populations within the nascent limb. These ligands stimulate signal transduction to cause structural and functional changes in cell behavior. This causes transcription factors to travel into the nucleus of the cell and create new DNA. [5] Both of these factors lead to the expression of new genes. Research continues to clarify the partially understood mechanisms of the molecular biology of the developing hand.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Development -- Development and Sex Determination of Gonads
retrieved_docs.content: The SRY gene encodes for a transcription factor that activates the testis-specific enhancer (TESCO) of a related autosomal gene known as SOX9 (SRY-Box Transcription Factor 9). The SOX9 gene is essential for the initiation of Sertoli cell differentiation from supporting cell precursors. These supporting cells would otherwise differentiate into follicle cells (specifically granuloma cells). [11] Besides SRY, there are other factors such as steroidogenic factor 1 (which is encoded by the gene NR5A1), which contribute to Sertoli cell differentiation. [12] SRY expresses exclusively in the supporting cells in the genital ridges, which differentiate into Sertoli cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Neural Tube -- Molecular Level -- Cilia
retrieved_docs.content: These are hair-like structures that help to regulate signal transduction with the use of mechanical molecular cues. Cilia are essential in major signaling pathways such as the sonic hedgehog pathway during neural tube formation. Approximately 5% of neural tube disorders have a relation to genes that are responsible for cilia formation and function. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Lanugo -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In the presence of an antagonistic Wnt signal, there is inhibition of the degradation complex, and β-catenin moves to the nucleus. There, it forms a transcription complex with DNA binding factors of the lymphoid enhancer-binding factor/T cell factor family. This complex then activates the transcription of various target genes. In the absence of an Shh signal, the actions of the patched-1 (PTC1) Shh receptor inhibit the activity of the smoothened (SMO) protein. In the presence of Shh, the repression of SMO is removed, and target genes, including PTC1 and GLI1 , are transcribed through the actions of the transcription factors GLI1 and GLI2 . After further downstream processes, lanugo and other types of hair formation occur. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Bowel -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Lgr5 + stem cells (leucine-rich repeat-containing G-protein coupled receptor 5) develop during the growth of intestinal villi and crypts and are essential for the biochemical balance of the intestine. These stem cells are also present in the formed intestine.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Weeks 6-8 -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Many precise molecular events guide normal organ system development. The gastrointestinal system, for instance, arises from the foregut. Hox genes are involved in specific molecular pathways for gut development. Hox gene expression is highly regulated and plays a role in determining the fate of different gut parts. For example, Hoxa3 and Hoxb4 genes have been found in the foregut, while the expression of Hoxc5 occurs in the midgut, and Hoxa13 genes are expressed in the hindgut. This gene expression pattern contributes to the digestive tract's molecular differentiation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: NR5A1 Receptor Mutations in this gene are the only monogenic mutation associated with spermatogenic and ovarian failure in 46 XY and 46 XX gonadal dysgenesis. NR5A1 has a two-zinc finger DNA binding domain and a 12-helix structure comparable to other nuclear receptors [9] . A recurrent point mutation has been documented in multiple individuals with ovarian failure and 46XX that stem from different families and different ethnicities. The mutation introduces a tryptophan residue preventing the protein from binding to the target DNA sequence. The current belief is that the NR5A1 receptor can interact with and activate beta-catenin. Beta-catenin is a regulator of sex development in multiple mammals and appears to need to be at certain levels for proper ovarian development, and when upregulated in an XY mammal can promote ovarian development while inhibiting proper testicular development [9] .

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: RNA helicase A (RHA) can function as a helicase for both RNA and DNA. The sequence and biochemical conservation of RHA and its homologs to humans suggests an evolutionarily conserved function. Normal gastrulation depends on RNA helicase A activity, as lack of proper RHA signaling results in ectodermal cell death with clear alterations in differentiation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Face -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The ectodermal placodes, which give rise to future sense organs and cranial ganglia, develop different molecular responses. In the placodes' anterior aspect, coding molecules express paired box protein (Pax) and the homeobox proteins Six3 (SIX3) and Otx2 (OTX2). Other molecules are present in the placodes' posterior portion, such as Iroquois-class homeodomain protein (IRX-1) and the homeobox protein Gbx2 (GBX-2). These proteins help regulate differentiation. For example, Pax6 is more concentrated in the olfactory and lens developmental areas, whereas Pax3 and Pax2/8 help form the trigeminal ganglia and auditory areas.

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, 2 additional genes, cyclin-dependent kinase-like 5 ( CDKL5 ) and forkhead box G1 ( FOXG1 ), are involved in the pathogenesis of RTT. The spectrum of mutation types includes missense, nonsense, and frameshift mutations. [7] [8] Mutations affecting the NLS region of MECP2 or early truncating mutations are associated with a more severe phenotype than missense mutations, whereas C-terminal deletions are linked to milder phenotypes. The R133C mutation is generally associated with a milder variant of RTT, often with preserved speech. The MeCP2 protein is responsible for 2 main functional roles: the methyl-binding domain, which specifically binds to the methylated CpG dinucleotides, and the transcription repression domain, which recruits repressor proteins that inhibit gene transcription. Mutations in an X-linked gene, CDKL5, have been reported in patients with a seizure variant of RTT. Notably, a wide spectrum of mutations associated with disease severity has been identified, and close to 30 different types of mutations can cause RTT. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Sexual Development -- Development -- Ovary
retrieved_docs.content: In a fetus with XX chromosomes, the gonads are initially indifferent and remain indifferent longer than in male gonadal development. Both autosomal genes and genes on the X chromosome contribute to ovary formation. Ovary-specific transcription factors induce ovarian differentiation; these include FOXL2 (forkhead transcription factor 2), WNT4 (wingless-type MMTV integration site family member 4), RSPO1 (R-spondin 1), and the beta-catenin pathway. [11] Only recently has the differentiation of the ovary been identified as an active and complex process. [12] It is now understood that the absence of an active SRY gene in a chromosomal male will not result in ovarian differentiation but in variable gonadal dysgenesis. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: In embryology, the set of permitted descriptors—referred to as “Allowed qualifier”—includes the entities بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, فالج, and قرحات الطرفين السفليين, indicating that these specific conditions are recognized as relevant qualifiers within the study and classification of embryologic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are associated with a uterine size for dates discrepancy in women who have a child, indicating that maternal health issues that affect gestation can manifest as mismatches between expected uterine size based on gestational age and the actual uterine measurements in mothers with existing offspring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as an abnormality of the amnion, indicating a deviation from normal amniotic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 5 -- Community 2954
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” designates embryology as a relevant descriptor for thyroid disorders, retinal detachment (تمزق الشبكية), and lower‑extremity ulcers (قرحات الطرفين السفليين). Recognizing embryology as an appropriate qualifier highlights the importance of developmental origins in understanding and managing these diverse conditions.
